## Patient Initial Report

**Patient Details**
- Age: 42-year-old
- Sex: Male

**Presenting Complaint**
- Polyuria
- Polydipsia
- Blurred vision
- Fatigue

**History**
- 10-year history of type 2 diabetes

**Vital Signs**
- Blood pressure: 140/90 mmHg

**Laboratory Values**
- Blood sugar: 350 mg/dl
- HbA1c: 10.5%

**Treatment Initiated**
- Intravenous fluids
- Insulin infusion
- Electrolyte replacement

**Extra Details**
- Fundoscopic exam reveals microaneurysms and cotton wool spots.

## Prelim Diagnosis Report

### 1. **Hyperosmolar Hyperglycemic State (HHS)**
**📌 Disease Description:**
- A serious complication of diabetes characterized by hyperglycemia, hyperosmolarity, and dehydration without significant ketoacidosis.

**🧠 Clinical Reasoning:**
- Blood sugar of 350 mg/dl and HbA1c of 10.5% indicate poor glycemic control.
- Symptoms of polyuria, polydipsia, and fatigue.
- Elevated blood pressure of 140/90 mmHg.

**✅ Recommendations:**
- Intravenous fluids
- Insulin infusion
- Electrolyte replacement

### 2. **Hypertensive Retinopathy**
**📌 Disease Description:**
- Damage to the retina due to high blood pressure.

**🧠 Clinical Reasoning:**
- Patient's blood pressure is 140/90 mmHg.

**✅ Recommendations:**
- Blood pressure control

### 3. **Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus with poor control**
**📌 Disease Description:**
- A metabolic disorder characterized by hyperglycemia due to insulin resistance and relative insulin deficiency.

**🧠 Clinical Reasoning:**
- 10-year history of type 2 diabetes.
- Elevated blood sugar and HbA1c levels indicate poor glycemic control.

**✅ Recommendations:**
- রক্তের গ্লুকোজ এবং HbA1c মাত্রা নিয়মিত পর্যবেক্ষণ করা। (Regular monitoring of blood glucose and HbA1c levels.)
- খাদ্যাভ্যাস এবং জীবনযাত্রার পরিবর্তন আনা। (Changes in diet and lifestyle.)
- ঔষধ সমন্বয় করা। (Medication adjustment.)## Best Practices Report

### **🩺 Hyperosmolar Hyperglycemic State (HHS)** [5 sources]
- **🔗 Source:** [https://www.diabetes.org.uk/sites/default/files/2022-02/JBDS+06+The+Management+of+Hyperosmolar+Hyperglycaemic+State+(HHS)+in+Adults+FINAL.pdf](https://www.diabetes.org.uk/sites/default/files/2022-02/JBDS+06+The+Management+of+Hyperosmolar+Hyperglycaemic+State+(HHS)+in+Adults+FINAL.pdf)
  - **📖 Summary:** HHS is a medical emergency, less frequent than DKA.

- **🔗 Source:** [https://my.clevelandclinic.org/health/diseases/21147-hyperosmolar-hyperglycemic-state](https://my.clevelandclinic.org/health/diseases/21147-hyperosmolar-hyperglycemic-state)
  - **📖 Summary:** HHS is a life-threatening complication of diabetes, mainly Type 2, resulting from prolonged high blood sugar, leading to severe dehydration and confusion. Stopping diabetes medications can lead to HHS. A complication of treatment is blood sugar dropping too quickly.

- **🔗 Source:** [https://doclibrary-rcht.cornwall.nhs.uk/DocumentsLibrary/RoyalCornwallHospitalsTrust/Clinical/EndocrineAndDiabetes/ManagementOfAdultHyperosmolarHyperglycaemicStateHHSClinicalGuideline.pdf](https://doclibrary-rcht.cornwall.nhs.uk/DocumentsLibrary/RoyalCornwallHospitalsTrust/Clinical/EndocrineAndDiabetes/ManagementOfAdultHyperosmolarHyperglycaemicStateHHSClinicalGuideline.pdf)
  - **📖 Summary:** HHS is a serious but rare medical emergency, typically in elderly patients with type 2 diabetes and multiple co-morbidities, associated with significant mortality. Increasingly reported in younger patients as an initial presentation of type 2 diabetes.

- **🔗 Source:** [https://emedicine.medscape.com/article/1914705-treatment](https://emedicine.medscape.com/article/1914705-treatment)
  - **📖 Summary:** IV insulin, similar dosages to DKA, can correct hyperglycemia in HHS. All patients experiencing HHS require intensive diabetes management, including insulin therapy.

- **🔗 Source:** [https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10107355/](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10107355/)
  - **📖 Summary:** HHS therapy aims to improve clinical status, replace fluid losses within 24 hours, gradually decline osmolality (3.0–8.0 mOsm/kg/h), achieve blood glucose of 10–15 mmol/L in the first 24 hours, and prevent complications like hypoglycemia, hypokalemia, VTE, osmotic demyelination, fluid overload, and foot ulceration.

### **🩺 Hypertensive Retinopathy** [5 sources]
- **🔗 Source:** [https://www.verywellhealth.com/hypertensive-retinopathy-3422174](https://www.verywellhealth.com/hypertensive-retinopathy-3422174)
  - **📖 Summary:** Diagnosed via eye exam by optometrists and ophthalmologists, involving dilation of the eye to examine internal structures.

- **🔗 Source:** [https://health.usnews.com/doctors/hypertensive-retinopathy](https://health.usnews.com/doctors/hypertensive-retinopathy)
  - **📖 Summary:** Recommends finding a hypertensive retinopathy doctor.

- **🔗 Source:** [https://www.healthcentral.com/condition/hypertension/hypertensive-retinopathy](https://www.healthcentral.com/condition/hypertension/hypertensive-retinopathy)
  - **📖 Summary:** Hypertensive retinopathy is damage to the retina caused by chronic high blood pressure.

- **🔗 Source:** [https://www.statpearls.com/point-of-care/35600](https://www.statpearls.com/point-of-care/35600)
  - **📖 Summary:** Hypertensive retinopathy (HR) occurs when retinal vessels are damaged due to poorly controlled hypertension (HTN), affecting cardiovascular, renal, cerebrovascular, and retinal systems.

- **🔗 Source:** [https://www.reviewofoptometry.com/article/crisis-unfolds-management-of-hypertensive-retinopathy](https://www.reviewofoptometry.com/article/crisis-unfolds-management-of-hypertensive-retinopathy)
  - **📖 Summary:** The Keith-Wagener-Barker scale grades hypertensive retinopathy. Grades 1 and 2 involve retinal vasculature changes due to chronic hypertension. Grades 3 and 4 result from sustained or acute malignant hypertension (BP > 180/120 mmHg).

### **🩺 Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus with poor control** [5 sources]
- **🔗 Source:** [https://bestpractice.bmj.com/topics/en-gb/24](https://bestpractice.bmj.com/topics/en-gb/24)
  - **📖 Summary:** Diagnosis based on two confirmed values: FPG ≥126 mg/dL (≥7.0 mmol/L), plasma glucose ≥200 mg/dL (≥11.1 mmol/L) 2 hours after 75 g oral glucose, or HbA1c ≥6.5% (≥48 mmol/mol). Alternatively, a single random plasma glucose ≥200 mg/dL (≥11.1 mmol/L) plus symptoms of hyperglycemia or hyperglycemic crisis.

- **🔗 Source:** [https://diabetesjournals.org/care/article/44/Supplement_1/S73/30909/6-Glycemic-Targets-Standards-of-Medical-Care-in](https://diabetesjournals.org/care/article/44/Supplement_1/S73/30909/6-Glycemic-Targets-Standards-of-Medical-Care-in)
  - **📖 Summary:** Risk factors include insulin use, poor glycemic control, albuminuria, and poor cognitive function. Level 3 hypoglycemia is associated with mortality.

- **🔗 Source:** [https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9304683/](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9304683/)
  - **📖 Summary:** Lists multiple studies examining factors associated with poor glycemic control in type 2 diabetes patients.

- **🔗 Source:** [https://pro.aace.com/clinical-guidance/diabetes](https://pro.aace.com/clinical-guidance/diabetes)
  - **📖 Summary:** AACE provides algorithms for clinical decision-making in type 2 diabetes management, promoting culturally appropriate language, health literacy, and community partnerships.

- **🔗 Source:** [https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8114074/](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8114074/)
  - **📖 Summary:** Insulin resistance and β-cell dysfunction are major hallmarks. Glucose resistance is influenced by fat cells, the gastrointestinal tract, α-cells, kidneys, and the brain. CCK is associated with leptin and insulin levels, disrupting glucose homeostasis.
